Q38: My family and I have been fishing at Harlan County Reservoir for over 50 years.  We have primarily targeted Wipers there since their introduction approximately 30 years ago.  I think Wipers have greatly benefitted the fishery, and I continue to be frustrated by the well-funded and organized walleye fisherman at Harlan despite studies (including the 3 year Kansas State biologist study) that show wipers do not affect the walleye fishery.  Recently, I've read and heard hints that make me wonder if the NPGC is placating the walleye group as wiper stocking levels and frequency have been reduced.  It's frustrating that this well-organized walleye group could be getting undue influence on the Harlan fishery. I fished Harlan over 150 days this year, and it was obvious that between late June and the end of September, that over 75% of the fisherman were targeting wipers and white bass, with large wipers being the primary goal.  The continued reduction or elimination of wipers from Harlan, would greatly hurt the local economy.  I also believe that wipers are vital to the health of the Harlan fishery by controlling the shad population.

I think the lakes in are state are beautiful and for the most part the people keep them in good condition, although I do wish more people were checked for licenses and caught illegally harvesting fish.  
I enjoy catching panfish, and bluegill and crappie fishing in Nebraska is second to none.  I like that I can ice fish during the winter and I also like hitting the local river for catfish in the summer.  The Sandhills lakes are extraordinary.  I love fishing for all sorts of fish at the Refuge.  I love that it is untouched by man for the most part and there are open prairies all around.
While I'm not a bass enthusiast, and really poor at fishing for them, I also know that at any time I can hook into a fat 18 inch bass in any lake in Nebraska.
F3:
We simply need more opportunities to catch yellow perch and walleye in Nebraska.  I have not gone across the border yet to catch them but this year or next I'm going to, and that could replace my one big trip of the year to the Sandhills.  I don't want it to.  I want to fish in Nebraska.  But you can only catch so many bluegills and 16 inch bass before you get bored with it.  I really, really wish I could catch more perch and walleye.  I do find the Northern Pike fishing excellent in Nebraska's western lakes, but you killed Pelican, which was the best Northern lake in the entire state, for more bluegill and not perch.  Can't go back on that one, I guess, so I won't complain any further about that.  
But you need to stop with the excuses regarding yellow perch and walleye.  There are too many private lake owners successfully growing those fish.  There are too many states around us with success.  We are tired of hearing Daryl's excuses as to why we simply can't have it in Nebraska, or that we can catch them if we just put 48 hours a week on the water.  Well, not all of us buying licenses have that much time to stalk fish until we finally catch some, and most of us don't live anywhere near driving distance for a day trip to actually fish a lake with more than a few of those fish in them.  Our licenses are costly enough; figure it out.  I fish at Summit and Maskentine for a regular trip, and will go to Wanahoo (not on a weekend, wow), or Czechland, and of course the Sandhills lakes.  If I could catch walleyes and/or perch at Summit or Maskentine I would be completely satisfied as the fishing for panfish and bass is already great there.
Thanks for asking for our opinions.

F3: As someone who grew up in Nebraska and has fished private and public water my entire life in Nebraska, I have a few recommendations.  I think that Nebraska Game and Parks should not spend so many resources on managing fish and fishing.  I think a more important use of resources is to manage the watershed, water quality, and the surrounding environment.  Personally, I think almost all fishing in Nebraska should be catch and release, the numbers and size of fish would obviously increase if this policy were implemented.  I think resources such as stocking walleye in reservoirs, could be better used to improve habitat in more areas.  Also, acquiring more public lands that are open to hunting and fishing in Nebraska would bring in more sportsmen to the state.
